Baron-Cohen, Leslie, and Frith (1985) had proposed that autistic individuals have a specific impairment in **Theory of Mind** (ToM) -- the ability to attribute beliefs, desires, and intentions to others. If ToM is genuinely impaired in autism, and if RT is correct that communication depends on intention recognition, then RT should make *precise predictions* about which communicative phenomena would be difficult for autistic individuals at different levels of ToM ability.

**Brown & Levinson's politeness theory (1987).** All communication carries face-threat, and speakers manage face-threat with a graded set of politeness strategies (bald-on-record, positive politeness, negative politeness, off-record implicature, saying nothing). The amount of politeness work expected is a function of social distance, power, and imposition, and the calibration is highly variable across cultures, communities, and individuals. **Autistic communication is generally calibrated lower on the politeness scaffolding dimensions** (less hedging, less indirection, less ritual softening), and this calibration is not deficient communication — it is a valid setting on a scale with many valid settings (Crompton et al., 2020). Pragmatic packaging invalidation exploits the calibration mismatch by treating the autistic setting as the wrong setting.

Burke et al. (2004), applying the Prochaska transtheoretical model to leaving abuse, found that approximately **95% of women in their abused-women sample were in the precontemplation or contemplation stage at study entry** — meaning they were not yet ready to leave, even though they were in measurably abusive relationships. The implication: most survivors of intimate terrorism are not in a position to "just leave," even when they recognize what is happening, and the appropriate intervention has to match the stage they are actually in.

- Burke, J. G., et al. (2004). "Ending Intimate Partner Violence: An Application of the Transtheoretical Model." *American Journal of Health Behavior* 28(2): 122–133. — [PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/15058512/) [paywalled]

Cage and Troxell-Whitman's (2019) empirical work on masking contexts and costs is complementary. They documented that masking motivations cluster around assimilation pressures (conforming to non-autistic norms to access resources or avoid punishment) and protection pressures (avoiding specific negative consequences of being read as autistic). Both clusters are responses to environmental pressure, not free choices. The protective reframe -- masking is work done under pressure, not a choice freely made -- is a frame substitution in the Lakoff sense. It changes how the reader interprets the same behavior.

citations.bare: - **Cage & Troxell-Whitman (2019)** -- on the contexts and costs of camouflaging.

Cage and Troxell-Whitman's (2019) study of 262 autistic adults asked directly about the reasons for masking. The findings are pragmatically consequential: masking is not motivated by the autistic person's enjoyment of the performance. The most frequently cited reasons were stigma avoidance, employment protection, fear of judgment, and the practical need to access non-autistic settings safely. A much smaller number of participants described masking as a neutral or positive practice.

- Cage, E. & Troxell-Whitman, Z. (2019). "Understanding the reasons, contexts and costs of camouflaging for autistic adults." *Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders*, 49(5), 1899-1911. citations.bare: **Cheung et al. (2017)** investigated four types of presupposition -- existential, factive, lexical, and structural -- in school-age children with and without ASD (n=21 ASD, n=106 typically developing, mean age 8.8). Children with ASD showed a deficit in presupposition comprehension even after controlling for general language ability and non-verbal intelligence. This was one of the first studies to systematically test multiple presupposition types in an autistic population.
- [Cheung et al. (2020) - Comprehension of presupposition triggers in Cantonese-speaking children with and without ASD](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/02699206.2019.1673486)
urls: Cheung et al. (2020) - Comprehension of presupposition triggers in Cantonese-speaking children with and without ASD

citations.bare: **Cheung et al. (2020)** expanded the investigation to seven presupposition trigger types: definite descriptions, factive predicates, change-of-state verbs, implicative verbs, iteratives, temporal clauses, and counterfactual conditionals. Twenty-seven children with ASD (mean age 9.07) were compared with age-matched and language-matched typically developing peers. The key finding: children with ASD performed significantly worse than age-matched TD peers, but *similarly* to language-matched TD peers -- except for temporal clauses, where ASD children showed unique difficulty even after controlling for age, language, and non-verbal intelligence. This suggests that much of the presupposition difficulty in autism tracks with overall language development rather than being a specific pragmatic deficit, with temporal presuppositions as a notable exception.

citations.key: clark|1977|formalized this as the given n
citations.bare: Clark and Haviland (1977) formalized this as the **Given-New Contract**: an implicit agreement between speakers and listeners that the speaker will mark given information (information the listener can already identify) as given and new information (information the listener cannot yet identify) as new, and that the listener will use given information as an anchor to integrate new information into their existing mental model. Violations of this contract — presenting as "given" something the listener does not in fact know, or presenting as "new" something already established — create processing difficulty and can cause communication breakdowns.

- **Crompton et al. (2020)**: Autistic people share information with other autistic people **as effectively as** neurotypical people do with each other. In mixed groups, transfer drops. The communication system works; the **mismatch** is the problem.

**Crompton et al. (2020)** demonstrated this experimentally: "Autistic people share information with other autistic people as well as non-autistic people do with other non-autistic people." Information transfer degraded only in mixed autistic/non-autistic groups, and rapport was higher in same-neurotype pairs (*Autism*, 24(7), 1901-1912).
**Crompton et al. (2020) - Diffusion Chain Study:**
- Crompton, C. J., DeBrabander, K., Heasman, B., Milton, D., & Sasson, N. J. (2020). "Double Empathy: Why Autistic People Are Often Misunderstood." *Frontiers for Young Minds* 8: 554875.

Crompton et al. (2020) established that autistic peer-to-peer communication is highly effective, and the finding has a specific application here: autistic peers can recognize the credentials/behavior gap more quickly than allistic friends can. The reasons are structural. Autistic peers have usually lived through versions of the pattern themselves. They process the credentials without the cultural script of *allies as the good guys* pre-loading their assessment. They are not subject to the same good-faith assumption about allyship statements because they have usually been burned by the assumption before. And they are not processing the specific relationship through the scarcity-of-allies frame in the same way, because their own network of autistic peers usually provides the support that an allistic friend's claimed allyship would have to provide from scratch.

citations.key: crompton|2020|found that autistic peer to pe
citations.bare: Crompton et al. (2020) found that autistic peer-to-peer information transfer is more effective than mixed autistic-allistic communication. Information flows accurately between autistic people in ways that often break down at the autistic-allistic interface. The applied implication for manipulation recognition is direct: **autistic peers are the single most effective reality-check resource available to an autistic target.** Other autistic people who have experienced similar patterns recognize them faster, communicate about them more accurately, and provide validation in a register that the target can actually use.

Crompton, C. J., Hallett, S., Ropar, D., Flynn, E., & Fletcher-Watson, S. (2020). "I never realised everybody felt as happy as I do when I am around autistic people": A thematic analysis of autistic adults' relationships with autistic and neurotypical friends and family. *Autism*, 24(6), 1438-1448. [SAGE](https://doi.org/10.1177/1362361320908976)

Crompton et al. (2020) identified autistic peer-to-peer communication as unusually effective for information transfer. The practical application here is that autistic peers are your most efficient and most accurate source of pattern recognition for what is happening to you. Autistic people who have experienced similar patterns will recognize the bandwagon move faster than non-autistic observers will, will communicate their recognition in a register you can actually use, and will not apply the pre-loaded credibility discount to your account.

Crompton et al. (2020): Information transfer is equally effective in autistic-autistic and NT-NT pairs. Common ground works FINE within neurotype. The breakdown is specifically at the neurotype boundary.

(2020) on autistic peer-to-peer communication** establishes the empirical basis for why autistic peer reality-check is particularly useful for this pattern: autistic peers can often recognize the credentials/behavior gap more quickly than allistic friends can, because they have usually lived through it and because they do not process the credentials through the cultural script of *allies as the good guys.*

- **Crompton, Hallett, et al. (2020)** on rapport in autistic-autistic versus mixed interactions. This study found that autistic adults reported significantly higher rapport, ease, and enjoyment when interacting with other autistic adults than when interacting in mixed pairs. The rapport finding is particularly important for stance theory because rapport is precisely the interactional outcome that stance alignment is supposed to produce. When rapport is high in autistic-autistic pairs, it means that the stance alignment work is landing — that each speaker's stance moves are being read the way they were intended.
- Crompton et al. (2020). — [PMC7545656](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7545656/)

**Crompton et al. (2020)** provided the key demonstration that autistic-to-autistic information transfer is highly effective -- in fact, as effective as non-autistic-to-non-autistic transfer, and more effective than mixed-group transfer. Participants played a "telephone game" (chained retellings of a story) in autistic-only chains, non-autistic-only chains, and mixed chains. Information loss was similar in the two same-group conditions and significantly higher in the mixed condition. The finding is structurally incompatible with an autistic-deficit account: if autism were a deficit in communication, autistic-to-autistic communication should be the worst of the three conditions. Instead, it is tied for best. The communication breakdown is a mismatch phenomenon, not a one-sided failure.
- [Crompton et al. (2020) - SAGE Journals](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/full/10.1177/1362361320919286) | [PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7545656/)

- Crompton et al. (2020) showed that "autistic individuals communicate more successfully and experience higher levels of rapport when interacting with other autistic individuals" -- autistic communication is effective *within its own community*.

Crompton et al. (2020) showed that autistic-to-autistic communication is highly effective — *more* effective in their experimental design than mixed autistic-allistic communication and comparable to allistic-to-allistic communication. The implication is that autistic communication is not deficient communication. It is communication calibrated for different conventions. When it operates with shared conventions, it works. When it operates across conventions, friction occurs — and the friction is bidirectional, not the autistic speaker's failure alone (Milton's double empathy problem).
- Crompton et al. (2020) showed that information transfer is equally effective in autistic-autistic and NT-NT pairs, but drops in mixed pairs.

**Crompton et al. (2020):** The diffusion chain study demonstrated that "autistic people share information with other autistic people as well as non-autistic people do with other non-autistic people." The breakdown occurs specifically in mixed neurotype groups.
**Crompton et al. (2020)** used a "telephone game" methodology with chains of autistic, non-autistic, and mixed pairs. Findings:

**DeBrabander, Morrison, Jones, Faso, Chmielewski, and Sasson (2019)** showed that first-impression biases against autistic people (based on thin-slice video) are driven by the *non-autistic* raters' judgments; autistic raters do not show the same bias. The frame that "autistic people are hard to read" is itself a frame held disproportionately by non-autistic observers, and it is based on mismatch effects rather than on absolute qualities of the autistic people being rated.

citations.key: debrabander|2019|thin slice work ran the study
citations.bare: DeBrabander et al.'s (2019) thin-slice work ran the study in the opposite direction: when autistic raters evaluated thin-slice videos of autistic speakers, they did not show the negative first-impression bias that non-autistic raters showed. The autistic raters were rating the same speakers more accurately, or at least more charitably, because they were not bringing the non-autistic frame-repertoire to the task.

- **Douglas & Sedgewick (2024)**: Domestic violence prevalence among autistic adults: 23.7%. Physical violence: 56-58.5%. Sexual violence: 31-56.8%. Stalking and harassment: 58.5%. Published in *Autism*, 28(7).

- Douglas, S., & Sedgewick, F. (2024). "Experiences of interpersonal victimization and abuse among autistic people." *Autism* 28(7): 1732–1745. PMC11191373. (Weaponized features framework: autism-related traits used as components of manipulation tactics, including concern-framed moves.)

- Douglas & Sedgewick (2024) found *no gender protection* — autistic men in their sample experienced sexual violence and IPV at rates similar to autistic women and non-binary participants. This is a significant deviation from the general population pattern.

Douglas & Sedgewick (2024), in a qualitative interview study of 24 autistic adults, identified six themes in autistic people's experiences of interpersonal victimization. One of those themes — **autism used against you** — became the core of their conceptual contribution: the *weaponized features* framing.

Douglas & Sedgewick (2024), in their qualitative study of 24 autistic adults' experiences of interpersonal victimization, identified the *weaponized features* framing: perpetrators deliberately exploit specific autism-related features as components of their tactics. The features list includes executive function dependencies, advance-information reliance, the tendency toward literal and good-faith interpretation, and the credibility discount. Every item on the list contributes to the information-control vulnerability this page addresses.

Douglas & Sedgewick (2024) introduced the *weaponized features* framing as a conceptual contribution from autistic-led qualitative research. The argument: perpetrators of abuse against autistic people often appear to **deliberately** exploit autism-related features as components of their tactics. The features are not the cause of the abuse; the *exploitation* of the features is the tactic.
- Douglas & Sedgewick (2024). — [PMC11191373](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11191373/)

- **Douglas & Sedgewick's (2024) weaponized features framework** — *"autism used against you"* — applies structurally: the autistic person's willingness to extend trust to someone who has signaled autism-affirmation is itself a feature that can be weaponized. The credentials become a tool in the harmer's kit rather than a reliable indicator of safety.

citations.key: frazier|2009|critique much of the ptg liter
citations.bare: - **Frazier et al. (2009)** critique: much of the PTG literature relies on retrospective self-report, which has known biases. When growth is measured prospectively (comparing before-trauma and after-trauma measures directly), the effect is much smaller than retrospective studies suggest. Some of what people report as "growth" may be reframing of how they remember their pre-trauma selves, not actual change.

- Frazier, P., et al. (2009). "Does self-reported posttraumatic growth reflect genuine positive change?" *Psychological Science* 20(7): 912–919. — Critical evaluation of retrospective self-report bias in PTG research.

- **Griffiths, Allison, Kenny, Holt, Smith & Baron-Cohen (2019)** developed the **Vulnerability Experiences Quotient (VEQ)**, a 60-item measure covering ten domains of interpersonal vulnerability. In a sample of 426 autistic adults and 268 non-autistic adults, autistic respondents reported higher rates on the majority of VEQ items. Group differences in anxiety, depression, and life satisfaction were partially mediated by total VEQ score — that is, part of the reason autistic adults in this sample reported worse mental health and lower life satisfaction is that they had actually experienced more adverse interpersonal events. The paper specifically highlights domestic abuse, involvement with social services as a parent, and financial exploitation and hardship as understudied domains of autistic vulnerability.

Griffiths et al. (2019) established that autistic adults have higher rates of social isolation as a baseline condition. The relevant point for bandwagon isolation: **a smaller starting network means a shorter distance to functional isolation, and fewer opportunities to cross-reference the manipulator's claims.** If you have three close relationships and the manipulator has been pre-loading all three, there are no outside voices left. If you have three close relationships and two are old autistic peer friendships that the manipulator is framing as "enabling," the available reality-check collapses to one person, who may themselves be inside the manipulator's social orbit.
- **Griffiths et al. (2019)** on the Vulnerability Experiences Quotient in autistic adults.

**Gunraj et al. (2016)** conducted experiments in which participants ranked the sincerity of text messages and handwritten letters that included final periods. Participants ranked text messages ending with a period as less sincere — but handwritten notes ending with a period as more sincere. This demonstrates that the pragmatic value of punctuation is **medium-specific**: the same mark carries different meanings in different contexts.

- Gunraj, D. N., et al. (2016). "Texting insincerely: The role of the period in text messaging." *Computers in Human Behavior*, 55, 1067-1075. [ScienceDirect (paywalled)](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chb.2015.11.003)

- Harsey, S. J., Zurbriggen, E. L. & Freyd, J. J. (2017). Deny, attack, and reverse victim and offender (DARVO): What is the influence on perceived perpetrator and victim credibility? *Journal of Aggression, Maltreatment & Trauma*, 29(8), 897-916.

- **Harsey, Zurbriggen, & Freyd (2017)**, *Journal of Aggression, Maltreatment & Trauma* 26(6): 644–663. The first major empirical study. Asked adult participants who had confronted someone about wrongdoing to describe the response they received. DARVO behaviors were common in the confrontation responses, and exposure to DARVO was associated with increased *victim self-blame*. The mechanism is direct: when the perpetrator's response moves from denying the act to attacking the victim's character and reframing themselves as the wronged party, the victim is more likely to internalize the blame than to maintain their original assessment.

- Harsey, S. J., Zurbriggen, E. L., & Freyd, J. J. (2017). "Perpetrator responses to victim confrontation: DARVO and victim self-blame." *Journal of Aggression, Maltreatment & Trauma* 26(6): 644–663. — [Tandfonline](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/10926771.2017.1320777) [paywalled]

- Harsey, S. J., & Freyd, J. J. (2020). "Deny, Attack, and Reverse Victim and Offender (DARVO): What Is the Influence on Perceived Perpetrator and Victim Credibility?" *Journal of Aggression, Maltreatment & Trauma* 29(8): 897–916. — [Tandfonline](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/10926771.2020.1774695) [paywalled]

- **Harsey & Freyd (2020)**, *Journal of Aggression, Maltreatment & Trauma* 29(8): 897–916. Tested whether DARVO affects how *third parties* perceive credibility. Result: when participants were shown perpetrator accounts that used DARVO, they rated the perpetrator as more believable and the victim as less believable. The effect was moderated by education — when participants were briefly informed about DARVO before reading the accounts, the persuasive effect of DARVO was reduced. **Knowing about DARVO partially inoculates third parties against being convinced by it.** This is one of the few empirical findings in the manipulation literature that supports a direct educational intervention.

- Harsey, S. J., & Freyd, J. J. (2022). Continuing empirical work refining the operational definition of DARVO. (See [Freyd Dynamics Lab publications](https://dynamic.uoregon.edu/jjf/articles/), for the current state of the literature.)
- **Harsey & Freyd (2022)**, follow-up empirical work refining the operational definition.

- **Heasman & Gillespie (2018)** on "neurodivergent intersubjectivity," arguing that autistic interactants develop specific coordinated strategies for shared understanding that look different from non-autistic strategies but are functionally equivalent.

Heasman and Gillespie's (2019) conversation-analytic study of autistic-autistic interaction documented high levels of mutual accommodation, genuine back-and-forth, and turn-taking patterns that matched what the participants themselves described as comfortable and generative. The finding contradicts stereotypes of autistic interaction as one-sided or interview-like. What Heasman and Gillespie actually observed was cooperative, frame-coordinated interaction in which both participants were evidently reading each other's frames successfully.
**Heasman & Gillespie (2019):** Identified two features of "neurodivergent intersubjectivity":

Heasman, B., & Gillespie, A. (2019). Neurodivergent intersubjectivity: Distinctive features of how autistic people create shared understanding as observed in autistic conversations about Minecraft. *Autism*, 23(4), 910–921. https://doi.org/10.1177/1362361318785172
- **Heasman & Gillespie (2019)** qualitative work on perspective-taking in autistic family interactions.
Heasman & Gillespie (2019) — see §5 below — specifically studied **collaborative video gaming** among autistic adults and found distinctive features of intersubjectivity emerging in that context.

**Heasman and Gillespie (2019)** used conversation analysis to show that autistic-autistic interactions display high levels of mutual accommodation and genuine back-and-forth, contrary to stereotypes of autistic talk as one-sided or interview-like.

"Putting on my best normal": Social camouflaging in adults with autism spectrum conditions. *Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders*, 47(8), 2519-2534. [PMC5509825](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5509825/)

**Hull et al. (2017, PMC5509825)** introduced the CAT-Q (Camouflaging Autistic Traits Questionnaire) and documented that masking — the conscious modulation of communication and affect to appear less autistic — imposes substantial cognitive and emotional costs. **Hull et al. (2019)** deepened this with qualitative work on the reasons, contexts, and costs of camouflaging. **Cook et al. (2021)** conducted a systematic review that found camouflaging to be consistently associated with anxiety, depression, burnout, and the erosion of self-knowledge. [VALIDATED — one of the strongest consensus findings in contemporary autism research]

Hull et al. (2019) developed the Camouflaging Autistic Traits Questionnaire (CAT-Q) through exploratory and confirmatory factor analysis with 354 autistic and 478 non-autistic adults. The final 25-item instrument has three factors, each of which corresponds to a distinct kind of stance work:

Hull, L., Mandy, W., Lai, M.-C., Baron-Cohen, S., Allison, C., Smith, P., & Petrides, K. V. (2019). Development and validation of the Camouflaging Autistic Traits Questionnaire (CAT-Q). *Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders*, 49(3), 819-833. [PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6394785/)

**Lim, Young & Brewer (2022)** and surrounding literature.

- Lim, A., Young, R. L., & Brewer, N. (2022). "Autistic Adults May Be Erroneously Perceived as Deceptive and Lacking Credibility." PMC8813809. (The observer effect that creates the pushback paradox: refusing the concern sounds like exactly what a worried person would say about.)
- Lim, Young & Brewer (2022) — Autistic Adults Perceived as Deceptive — [PMC8813809](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8813809/)

- **Lim, Young & Brewer's (2022) credibility discount** documents that when an autistic person pushes back against a non-autistic interlocutor, third-party observers default to believing the interlocutor over the autistic person. When the interlocutor also carries publicly visible allyship credentials, the default is compounded: observers now have a second reason — *they're an ally, so surely they wouldn't* — to disbelieve the autistic person.

Lim, Young & Brewer (2022) documented that autistic adults are perceived as less credible and more deceptive by non-autistic observers, an effect that operates below conscious awareness. For concern trolling, the credibility discount creates a specific trap: **when the autistic person pushes back against concern framing, the pushback is heard by observers — and sometimes by the autistic person themselves, operating on the internalized observer model — as defensiveness, instability, or paranoia. The pushback then "confirms" the concern.**

Lim, Young & Brewer (2022) documents that autistic adults are perceived as less credible and more deceptive by non-autistic observers, even when telling the truth. The effect is below conscious awareness and reflects how observers process affect, prosody, gaze, and fluency. For performative allyship specifically, the credibility discount has a compounded effect: when the autistic person tries to push back against a self-identified ally, third-party observers default to believing the ally over the autistic person, and the *they are an ally* frame gives them a second independent reason on top of the default credibility discount.

Lim, Young & Brewer (2022) found autistic adults are perceived as more deceptive and less credible than neurotypical adults *when telling the truth*. This has a consequence that is easy to miss: if you calibrate your sense of whether you're "overreacting" by watching how others react to your descriptions of what happened, you are calibrating to a biased instrument. Other people's skepticism of your account is partly a fact about their calibration, not about your accuracy.

Lim, Young & Brewer (2022) found that autistic adults are perceived as more deceptive and less credible than non-autistic adults *even when telling the truth*. The same study and the surrounding literature suggest that autistic communication features (gaze aversion, flat or different affect, literal phrasing) contribute to this perception even though they are not in fact reliable indicators of deception. **The implication for tone policing: even when an autistic person delivers a concern in a way they consider careful and measured, listeners are likely to perceive it as more aggressive, more hostile, or more inappropriate than the speaker intended.** The gap between intended delivery and perceived delivery is not the speaker's failure — it is a product of pre-loaded interpretation patterns in the listener.

Lim, Young & Brewer (2022) found that disclosing autism reduced the deception attribution in mock-suspect studies — the credibility discount diminished when observers had a name for the unusual features they were seeing. This is a hopeful finding and it is real.

Lim, Young & Brewer (2022), in their paper "Autistic Adults May Be Erroneously Perceived as Deceptive and Lacking Credibility," documented that autistic adults are perceived as less credible and more deceptive than non-autistic adults in observer studies, even when the autistic person is telling the truth. The effect is not moral — it operates below conscious awareness and reflects how observers read cues like affect, prosody, gaze, and fluency.

citations.key: lim|2022|in their paper on the autistic
citations.bare: Lim, Young & Brewer (2022), in their paper on the autistic credibility discount, documented that autistic adults are perceived as less credible and more deceptive by non-autistic observers, even when they are telling the truth. The effect operates below conscious awareness and reflects how observers read affect, prosody, gaze, and fluency. For concern trolling, the credibility discount has a specific consequence: **the autistic target's attempt to push back against the concern wrapper is heard, by outside observers, as defensiveness, instability, or paranoia.** The pushback itself then becomes new evidence *for* the concern. The speaker says *"I'm worried about you."* The autistic person says *"I don't think that's what's happening."* The observer, subject to the credibility discount, hears the denial as exactly the kind of thing a worried person would say about. The loop closes.

citations.key: lim|2022|is the single most load bearin
citations.bare: Lim, Young & Brewer (2022) is the single most load-bearing empirical fact for this page. The bandwagon chorus does not need to be carefully constructed against an autistic target because the audience already defaults to believing the non-autistic version. A manipulator can claim a chorus and the claim will be absorbed by outside observers — and, in a more insidious way, by the autistic target themselves — because the cultural default that autistic people are unreliable observers pre-licenses the claim. Against a non-autistic target, the manipulator might have to produce some actual social scaffolding to make the bandwagon land. Against an autistic target, the scaffolding is already in place. The cost of the move is lower, and the return is higher.
- Lim, Young & Brewer (2022). *JADD* 52(2):490-507. — [PMC8813809](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8813809/)

Lim, Young & Brewer (2022) on the credibility discount applies to social services assessments with particular force. Assessors are making formal evaluative judgments under time pressure, and the features that produce the credibility discount in other contexts produce it in this one too:
- Lim, Young & Brewer (2022). — [PMC8813809](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8813809/)

Lim, Young & Brewer (2022) showed that autistic adults are perceived as more deceptive and less credible than non-autistic adults, even when telling the truth. The compliance trap interacts with this finding in a particular way: the autistic person who reports that they cannot sustain the demanded masking is met with disbelief proportional to the credibility discount. "You can do it, you're just not trying hard enough" is the predictable response from an audience that already discounts the autistic person's account of their own capacity.

citations.key: lim|2022|study is the most direct empir
citations.bare: Lim, Young, and Brewer's (2022) study is the most direct empirical demonstration of the credibility discount. The design is important for understanding what the result actually shows. The researchers recorded 30 autistic and 29 neurotypical adults in video interviews in which each participant was asked to tell both a truthful account and a deceptive account on matched topics. The videos were coded for a set of candidate autistic behavioral features: gaze aversion, repetitive body movements, literal interpretation, poor reciprocity, and flat affect. A separate set of 1,410 participants then viewed a single video each and rated the interviewee's truthfulness and credibility.

- Lim, A., Young, R. L., & Brewer, N. (2022). Surrounding empirical work on perceived deception in autistic mock-suspects, including the finding that diagnostic disclosure reduces the credibility discount when received in good faith.

Locher and Watts (2005) propose **relational work** as the umbrella concept: all the work that participants do to negotiate their relationships in interaction. Politeness is one part of relational work; impoliteness is another part; politic behavior is another; ritualized behavior is another. The analyst's job is to study relational work in its full range, not just the narrow slice that Brown and Levinson focused on.

- Malkin and Abbot-Smith (2021) found autistic children were over-informative even in simple conditions, before any additional cognitive demands were introduced

- Malkin & Abbot-Smith (2021) found that autistic children were less likely to provide a context-appropriate amount of information overall than neurotypical peers. Critically, when object re-description was required (switching from calling something "the cup" to "the big cup" when context changed), autistic children only produced an appropriate description half the time. Without the need to re-describe, they could take listener needs into account.

- **Mazzaggio, Foppolo, Job & Surian (2021)** tested 26 children with ASD (ages 4-10, mean 7.1) and 26 TD controls on both scalar and ad-hoc implicatures. Children with ASD showed difficulty with **both** types, but the predictors differed: **Theory of Mind predicted performance on scalar but not ad-hoc implicatures**, while **IQ modulated performance on both types**. This suggests different mechanisms underlie different implicature types. Published in *Journal of Communication Disorders*. PubMed ID: 33621945

citations.bare: **McClanahan, Maner, and Cheng (2022)** published "Two Ways to Stay at the Top: Prestige and Dominance Are Both Viable Strategies for Gaining and Maintaining Social Rank Over Time" in *Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin*. Studying 548 MBA students in 104 project groups, they found that peer-rated dominance and prestige **both** predicted gains in social rank over 4 weeks — indicating that both strategies help people not only gain but also maintain status over time.

citations.bare: - **Mitchell, Sheppard, & Cassidy (2021)** extended the line of work, arguing that the phenomena traditionally called "autistic social impairment" are better characterized as an interaction-level mismatch.

citations.key: mitchell|2021|in autism and the double empat
citations.bare: **Mitchell, Sheppard, and Cassidy (2021)**, in "Autism and the double empathy problem: Implications for development and mental health" (*British Journal of Developmental Psychology* 39(1):1-18), review the evidence linking this systematic mis-framing of autistic adults by non-autistic observers to mental health outcomes. Their argument: being chronically misperceived is associated with poorer mental health and wellbeing in autistic adults, and the misperception is not a failing of autistic people but a consequence of the double empathy problem. The paper is explicit that the frame of autism held by the non-autistic majority is itself contributing to mental health harm, and that attempting to address the mental health outcomes without addressing the frame misses the causal structure.

- **Morrison et al. (2020)** on first impressions between autistic and non-autistic participants.

Morrison, K. E., DeBrabander, K. M., Jones, D. R., Faso, D. J., Ackerman, R. A., & Sasson, N. J. (2020). Outcomes of real-world social interaction for autistic adults paired with autistic compared to typically developing partners. *Autism*, 24(5), 1067-1080. [SAGE](https://doi.org/10.1177/1362361319892701)

Morrison et al.'s (2020) work on real-world interaction outcomes extended the finding beyond the laboratory: autistic adults paired with other autistic adults rated interactions more positively, reported lower social effort, and showed behavioral measures consistent with easier interaction than in mixed pairings. The non-autistic participants in the mixed pairings reported no clear difference, which is itself diagnostic: the asymmetry of effort is being absorbed by the autistic participant in mixed settings.

- Pearson, A., & Rose, K. (2021). A conceptual analysis of autistic masking: Understanding the narrative of stigma and the illusion of choice. *Autism in Adulthood*, 3(1), 52-60. DOI: 10.1089/aut.2020.0043. [PMC8992880](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8992880/)

Pearson and Rose's (2021) conceptual analysis is the foundational reframe paper for the whole area. Before their analysis, the camouflaging literature was largely framed in clinical terms: masking as an observable phenomenon to be measured, explained by gender differences, late diagnosis, or cognitive compensation. Pearson and Rose argue that this framing misses the mechanism. Masking, they argue, is a stigma-driven adaptation to an environment that penalizes autistic defaults, not a cognitive strategy that autistic people happen to deploy.

Pearson and Rose's (2021) conceptual analysis of autistic masking in *Autism in Adulthood* extends the masking literature in a direction that is particularly relevant to frame analysis. They argue that masking is often described as a "choice" autistic people make, and they show that the "choice" framing is empirically inaccurate and functionally harmful. Masking is, on their analysis, a response to a stigma environment in which unmasked autistic presentation is punished. Calling it a choice installs a frame in which the autistic person is responsible for the costs of masking, rather than a frame in which the environment is responsible for creating the conditions under which masking was necessary.
- **Pearson & Rose (2021)** -- critical conceptual review of camouflaging research and its relationship to masking.

Pearson and Rose (2021) ground their reframe in social identity theory (Tajfel and Turner) and in Goffman's sociology of stigma. This grounding matters because it provides a vocabulary for the autistic person's experience that is not clinical. In social identity theory, members of stigmatized groups engage in predictable stigma-management strategies: concealment, selective disclosure, in-group affiliation, and the cultivation of positive group identity as a counter to external devaluation. Each of these strategies has been documented in minority communities of many different kinds. The reframe locates autistic masking inside this general pattern rather than treating it as an autism-specific peculiarity.

Pearson et al. (2022) and Douglas & Sedgewick (2024) both document this pattern in qualitative studies of autistic adults' experiences of intimate partner abuse. Pearson's interviewees described "feeling like they deserved it because they were autistic" — the internal endpoint of diagnostic gaslighting, where the discrediting frame is taken inside and operates without external prompting. Diagnostic Weaponization gets its own page (Tier 3) because the "weaponized features" framing from Douglas & Sedgewick deserves its own treatment.

citations.key: pearson|2022|this was just how this friends
citations.bare: - Pearson, A., Rees, J., & Forster, S. (2022). "This was just how this friendship worked": Experiences of interpersonal victimization among autistic adults. *Autism in Adulthood*, 4(2), 141-150. [PMC9645672](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9645672/) — DOI: 10.1089/aut.2021.0035

Pearson, Rees, & Forster (2022) titled their qualitative study **"'This Was Just How This Friendship Worked': Experiences of Interpersonal Victimization Among Autistic Adults"** because the phrase recurred in their interviews. The phrase captures a specific autism-relevant pattern: the autistic adult, looking back on a friendship that was clearly exploitative in retrospect, describes their experience inside the friendship as treating the exploitative dynamics as normal — "this was just how this friendship worked," with the implicit understanding that the autistic person had assumed all friendships might work this way and therefore did not question it.

citations.key: pearson|2023|and related work on autism and
citations.bare: Pearson et al. (2023) and related work on autism and interpersonal victimization document what autistic community accounts have long reported: autistic adults reporting abuse, harassment, or misconduct are often not believed, or are believed less credibly than non-autistic reporters of similar reports. The pattern is not universal but it is well enough documented that it has to be treated as a structural feature of many institutional response contexts rather than as individual bad luck.
- Pearson et al. (2023) — autistic survivors had few trusted others to reality-check their experiences against.

citations.key: pearson|2023|i felt like i deserved it beca
citations.bare: - Pearson, A., Rose, K., & Rees, J. (2023). 'I felt like I deserved it because I was autistic': Understanding the impact of interpersonal victimisation in the lives of autistic people. *Autism*, 27(2), 500-511. [SAGE Journals](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/abs/10.1177/13623613221104546)

- **Pearson, Rose, & Rees (2023)** in *Autism* surveyed and interviewed 102 autistic adults about their experiences of interpersonal violence and victimization. Four themes emerged: (1) "the usual for autism" — participants had come to expect victimization as a routine part of being autistic; (2) "personhood revoked" — the cost of masking and the erosion of self that accompanies sustained victimization; (3) "unpacking the baggage" — the delayed recognition that what had happened to them was actually wrong; and (4) structural inequality and the difficulty of accessing support. The title phrase — "I felt like I deserved it because I was autistic" — encodes a pattern the quantitative work cannot capture: the internalized belief that being autistic made the victimization appropriate, reasonable, or inevitable.

citations.key: pearson|2023|in their paper i felt like i d
citations.bare: Pearson, Rose, & Rees (2023), in their paper *"'I felt like I deserved it because I was autistic,'"* documented the installed conviction that many autistic adults carry — that their autism makes them excessive, burdensome, or in need of the corrective attention of non-autistic others. The belief was not produced by any single abusive relationship. It was produced by a lifetime of cumulative messaging, much of it from adults who believed they were helping: *"I'm worried about you," "I think you need more help than you're asking for," "I'm saying this because I care about you."* The messaging installed the conviction that the autistic person's own sense of their state was unreliable and that external worriers were the authoritative source of the truth about them. By adulthood, the script is load-bearing.

- **Pearson et al.'s (2023) internalization findings** (*"I felt like I deserved it because I was autistic"*) document that autistic adults who have been harmed by people they initially trusted frequently arrive at the conviction that they are misreading the harm, and one of the factors that makes the misreading stick is the presence of allyship-adjacent features in the harmer's self-presentation.
- **Pearson et al. (2023)** on autism, trauma, and victimization.

Pearson et al. (2023)'s finding that autistic adults in abusive relationships often describe feeling they *"deserved it because I was autistic"* has a specific bandwagon-isolation form: **the chorus is installed inside you.** After enough exposure to "everyone agrees," the manipulator no longer has to invoke the chorus externally, because you are invoking it yourself. You are checking your own perceptions against the imagined chorus before speaking; you are doubting your own accounts because "everyone" already disagrees; you are refraining from reaching out to friends because "they all see it the same way." At the endpoint, the manipulator's labor has been outsourced to the target.
- Pearson, Rose, Rees (2023). — [Sage](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/abs/10.1177/13623613221104546) [paywalled]

citations.key: pearson|2023|titled their paper i felt like
citations.bare: Pearson et al. (2023) titled their paper "*'I felt like I deserved it because I was autistic'*" because that phrase, or close variants, recurred across their interview participants. The phrase is the endpoint of diagnostic weaponization successfully internalized: the autistic person no longer needs the perpetrator to discount them, because they are doing it themselves. The diagnostic frame has become the operating premise. Every concern is filtered through it before it ever reaches consciousness.

Pearson, Rose & Rees (2023) titled their qualitative study "'I felt like I deserved it because I was autistic'" because that phrase, or close variants, recurred across their interview participants. The phrase is the endpoint of a successful internalization: the autistic person no longer needs the abuser to discount them, because they are doing it themselves. The diagnostic frame has become the operating premise.

citations.key: pearson|2024|in it s not a physical prison
citations.bare: Pearson et al. (2024), in "'It's Not a Physical Prison but You Can't Get Out,'" documented that autistic adults in abusive intimate relationships frequently describe being trapped by the material and resource architecture of the relationship, not only by the emotional or coercive elements. The relevant point for this page: **in many of the survivor accounts in that paper, the material trap was partly an information trap.** Participants reported not knowing what was in joint accounts, not knowing what bills were owed, not knowing what the manipulator had been saying about them to family and friends, not knowing what their actual legal or financial situation was. The "can't get out" experience was produced in part by the inability to see the shape of the situation clearly enough to plan around it.

citations.bare: **Petit, Geoffray, Cassar & Baltazar (2025)**, in "Some (but not all) Pragmatic Inferences are Difficult for Autistic Children" (Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders), compared 23 autistic children with 237 neurotypical children. Autistic children showed poorer scalar implicature but similar metaphor comprehension -- the reverse of what many theories predicted. The authors proposed that a dimension opposing "guided" versus "spontaneous" pragmatic processes may explain when autistic individuals struggle.

Raymaker et al. (2020) defined autistic burnout as a distinct construct precisely because it does not map onto the existing categories of laziness, depression, or weakness. The chronic exhaustion is qualitatively different from depression. The skill loss is qualitatively different from forgetting or carelessness. The reduced sensory tolerance is not psychosomatic.

Raymaker et al. (2020) defined autistic burnout as characterized by chronic exhaustion, loss of skills, and reduced sensory tolerance, attributed to chronic life stress and capacity mismatch. The overlap between autistic burnout and complex trauma is significant:
Raymaker et al. (2020) defined autistic burnout from community-based participatory research with 19 interviews and 19 public online sources. The primary characteristics they identified:
- Raymaker, D. M. et al. (2020). Defining autistic burnout. *Autism in Adulthood*, 2(2), 132-143. [PMC7313636](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7313636/)
- Raymaker et al. (2020). Defining Autistic Burnout. — [PMC7313636](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7313636/)

citations.key: raymaker|2020|having all of your internal re
citations.bare: - Raymaker, D. M., et al. (2020). "'Having All of Your Internal Resources Exhausted Beyond Measure and Being Left with No Clean-Up Crew': Defining Autistic Burnout." *Autism in Adulthood* 2(2): 132–143. PMC7313636. (Reduced sensory tolerance and skill loss are primary characteristics; both are conditions that make meltdowns more frequent.)

Raymaker et al. (2020) on autistic burnout document what sustaining non-native frame-performance costs over time. The participants in the Raymaker study described burnout as resulting from *chronic life stress and a mismatch of expectations and abilities without adequate supports* -- and masking was one of the primary stressors. The costs include exhaustion, loss of skills, worsening physical and mental health, identity erosion, and, in severe cases, inability to sustain ordinary daily functioning. The costs are not metaphorical. They are well-documented empirical outcomes.

**Raymaker et al. (2020)** on autistic burnout provides the cost substrate: sustained masking is one of the primary precipitants of autistic burnout, a condition characterized by loss of skills, loss of executive function, and loss of the capacity for the masking itself. The burnout profile is the structural consequence of the third leg of the double bind operating across years. [VALIDATED]

Rumball et al. (2020) and the surrounding literature have documented that autistic adults often experience as traumatic a wider range of events than the DSM-5 Criterion A stressor definition recognizes. Sensory events specifically — being trapped in an overwhelming sensory environment, being forced to endure a sensory input that registers as dangerous, having a meltdown in a setting where it was not safe — can produce PTSD-like presentations even though they would not qualify as "trauma" under standard diagnostic criteria.

- Rumball et al. (2020) — autistic adults may experience as traumatic a wider range of events than DSM-5 recognizes, including routine changes, sensory overload, rule-breaking by others, and events that don't fit standard PTSD criteria.

- Rumball, F., Happé, F. & Grey, N. (2020). *Experience of Trauma and PTSD Symptoms in Autistic Adults: Risk of PTSD Development Following DSM-5 and Non-DSM-5 Traumatic Life Events.* Autism Research. https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1002/aur.2306

Schegloff and Sacks (1973) identified the defining properties of adjacency pairs:
Schegloff and Sacks (1973) identified this as the "closing problem": how do two people transition from "in conversation" to "not in conversation" without either party feeling cut off?

Schegloff and Sacks (1973), in their landmark paper "Opening Up Closings," identified the fundamental **closing problem**: any turn in a conversation could potentially be followed by another turn. The turn-taking system (Sacks, Schegloff, & Jefferson, 1974) distributes turns continuously -- after every turn, someone can speak next. This means there is no natural endpoint built into the system. You have to **create** the endpoint through cooperative work.
Schegloff and Sacks (1973), in their seminal paper "Opening up Closings," identified that ending a conversation requires a structured sequence, not just a sudden "bye":
- Schegloff, E.A. & Sacks, H. (1973). "Opening up Closings". [De Gruyter](https://doi.org/10.1515/semi.1973.8.4.289)

Schegloff and Sacks (1973) showed that conversation closings have a systematic, multi-stage structure:
- [Schegloff, Jefferson & Sacks (1977)](https://muse.jhu.edu/article/453740/summary)

citations.key: sperber|1981|used the term echoic mention b
citations.bare: Sperber and Wilson initially (1981) used the term "echoic mention" but later (1986, 2012) refined this to "echoic use," broadening the notion. They emphasized that the echo need not be of a specific prior utterance -- it can echo an expectation, a hope, a social norm, or any thought attributable to someone. This made the theory more flexible and able to handle cases where there is no obvious prior statement being echoed.
- Sperber and Wilson (1984) argued in response that pretense may describe parody well but fails to account for many types and properties of irony proper

1. Sperber and Wilson's (1986) Relevance Theory makes the role of **intention recognition** explicit in communication. Understanding what someone means requires recognizing what they *intended* to communicate -- and this requires the ability to attribute mental states.

Key finding: "A significantly greater proportion of autistic participants (50.7%) accurately interpreted the behavior of an autistic employee compared with non-autistic participants (31.2%)." This demonstrates that the "misreading" goes both ways -- non-autistic colleagues are *more likely* to misinterpret autistic behavior than autistic colleagues are.

Tannen and Wallat (1987) developed the concept of **interactive frames** in a landmark study of pediatric examinations. A pediatrician examining a child with cerebral palsy was simultaneously operating in multiple frames: the *examination* frame (with the child), the *consultation* frame (with the mother), the *teaching* frame (with observing students), and the *reporting* frame (dictating into a tape recorder). The pediatrician shifted frames constantly, cued by whom she was addressing, and each shift changed her vocabulary, tone, and stance.
- Tannen, D., & Wallat, C. (1987). Interactive frames and knowledge schemas in interaction. *Social Psychology Quarterly*, 50(2), 205-216. [JSTOR (paywalled)](https://doi.org/10.2307/2786752)

citations.bare: Thibodeau & Boroditsky (2011) *PLOS ONE* "Metaphors We Think With: The Role of Metaphor in Reasoning" ran a now-famous series of experiments on policy judgment. Participants read a brief passage about rising crime in a fictional city. In one condition, crime was described as "a beast preying on the city." In the other, crime was described as "a virus infecting the city." Everything else was held constant. Participants were then asked what the city should do about the problem.

(2025):** A systematic review and thematic meta-synthesis of 52 qualitative papers on autistic adults' experiences of interacting with other autistic people found that "many experiences of other autistic people were positive and were associated with improved Quality of Life across a range of domains." Autistic people describe contact with other autistic people as allowing them "to be their authentic selves."

- Watzlawick, P., Weakland, J., & Fisch, R. (1974). *Change: Principles of Problem Formation and Problem Resolution*. Norton. [Amazon](https://www.amazon.com/Change-Principles-Problem-Formation-Resolution/dp/0393707067)

Watzlawick, Weakland, and Fisch (1974) developed reframing into a core technique of brief therapy in their book *Change: Principles of Problem Formation and Problem Resolution*. Their insight was that many problems are sustained not by the underlying facts but by the frame that has been placed around the facts. If the frame changes, the problem either dissolves or becomes tractable. A child's "stubbornness" reframed as "persistence" is the same behavior evaluated from inside a different frame -- but the new frame makes different next-actions available. The therapeutic reframe does not deny the facts; it contests the frame in which the facts have been interpreted.

citations.bare: - **Zolyomi, Begel, Waldern, Tang, Barnett, Cutrell, McDuff, Andrist & Morris (2019). "Managing Stress: The Needs of Autistic Adults in Video Calling." *Proceedings of the ACM on Human-Computer Interaction*, 3(CSCW), Article 134** reports on qualitative interviews with autistic adults about video calling. Themes include: self-view as both useful (for monitoring masking) and distressing; preference for camera-off when stressed; difficulty with group video calls specifically; reported benefits of asynchronous alternatives.
